Authorship of supplementary conclusions II.41–51 is separated from the secure attribution of the Prologue, Part I, and Part II.1–40.
The local 1997 availability notice and U-M's current No Copyright–United States statement are recorded independently.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy or version of U-M CME item ChaucerAstr, keyed by OCR from Walter W. Skeat's 1872 EETS edition of Chaucer's Treatise on the Astrolabe. Skeat's text uses Cambridge University Library MS Dd.3.53 for the Prologue, Part I.1–23, and Part II.1–40, then appends conclusions from St John's College Cambridge MS E.2, Bodleian MS Digby 72, and Bodleian MS Bodley 619. Modern scholarship generally treats the conclusions after II.40 as supplementary and probably non-Chaucerian, while leaving the precise authentic boundary open.

catalogSummary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer; supplementary conclusions of uncertain authorship
catalogSummary.date: Core dated 1391; additions dated 1393 or later
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of composition not securely established
catalogSummary.title: A Treatise on the Astrolabe

catalogingSubject.label: A Treatise on the Astrolabe
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The Middle English prose treatise addressed to Lowys, comprising a Chaucerian core (Prologue, Part I.1–23, and Part II.1–40) plus eleven supplementary conclusions (Part II.41–51) of uncertain authorship as assembled in Skeat's 1872 edition and U-M CME item ChaucerAstr. Skeat's modern front matter and textual notes are excluded from the cataloged medieval composite.

contentParts.label: Prologue
contentParts.summary: Chaucer addresses ten-year-old Lowys, dates the work to 1391, explains his vernacular purpose, and outlines a planned five-part manual.

contentParts.label: Part I: Description of the Astrolabe
contentParts.summary: Twenty-three sections identify and explain the ring, mother, plates, rete, rule, alidade, scales, circles, stars, and other components of the instrument.

contentParts.label: Part II.1–40: Core Conclusions
contentParts.summary: Forty procedures use the instrument to determine celestial positions, time, latitude, ascensions, planetary conditions, and related quantities.

contentParts.label: Supplementary Conclusions II.41–51
notes: The XML node numbering is normalized to II.41–51, while several displayed headings reproduce alternate numbering such as 41a, 41b, 42a, and 42b.
contentParts.summary: Eleven additional procedures on shadows, heights, mean motion, and related calculations, printed from St John's E.2, Digby 72, and Bodley 619 and generally regarded as later supplements.

notes: The prologue explicitly identifies the year and Lowys's age; the plan described there was not fully completed in the surviving core.

dateStatements.display: 1393 or later additions in the textual tradition
notes: This dates additions associated with the textual tradition; it does not assign their authorship to Chaucer.

extent.description: Seventy-five cataloged content units: one unnumbered prologue plus 74 numbered instructional sections. Part I has 23 sections and Part II has 51, of which II.1–40 form the core and II.41–51 are supplementary. The XML's node sequence numbers all 51 Part II divisions even where Skeat's displayed supplement headings repeat 41–43-style numbers.

formStatements.description: A prose instructional treatise organized as a prologue, a description of the instrument, and numbered operational conclusions.

scope.description: Latin headings, terms, and phrases occur throughout the Middle English instructions

notes: Oxford is recorded as an operational associated place because the treatise uses its latitude; it is not asserted as the place of composition.
The XML's sequential nodes II.41–51 and Skeat's displayed supplement numbering are both preserved conceptually; users should cite the displayed heading when precision matters.
A four-witness edition with scoped supplements must not inherit a single manuscript's dialect or physical description at work level.

next_steps: Consult the Variorum textual introduction and current manuscript scholarship; continue to display the supplementary stratum separately.
openQuestions.question: Which, if any, conclusions after Part II.40 are Chaucer's?

next_steps: Use Eagleton's source analysis and the Variorum commentary to map source relationships section by section rather than naming one universal exemplar.
openQuestions.question: Which particular Latin exemplars and intermediary compilations underlie each part of Chaucer's core treatise?

next_steps: Consult current Chaucer biography and documentary scholarship before adding an authority record or biographical claims.
openQuestions.question: Can the addressee Lowys be independently identified beyond the text's description ‘my sone’ and age ten?

next_steps: Seek written clarification from U-M before redistributing the XML or a close derivative, especially through a for-profit service.
openQuestions.question: Do U-M's current NoC-US statement and access interface supersede the redistribution restriction embedded in the 1997 XML?

notes: This is not an assertion of an exact place of composition or a single scribal dialect.
Operational association with Oxford does not prove that the treatise was composed there.

scope.description: Part II uses Oxford as a practical latitude and place reference
Securely Chaucerian core through Part II.40
Translation and reworking of Latin astrolabe traditions
Vernacular adaptation organized for an individual learner and instrument

assertion.notes: Most scholarship treats these conclusions as non-Chaucerian additions, but the precise boundary and number of contributors remain uncertain.

notes: Do not display the whole 51-conclusion Part II as unqualified Chaucerian authorship.

scope.description: Printed text assembled from four specified manuscript witnesses

subjects.label: Medieval astrology and planetary calculation
notes: The text includes astrological terminology and procedures while explicitly distancing itself from some judicial practices.

summaries.value: A Middle English prose manual explaining the parts and operation of a planispheric astrolabe for the learner Lowys. The core describes the instrument and gives numbered procedures for finding solar and stellar positions, time, latitude, ascensions, planetary positions, and related astronomical quantities. Skeat's edition appends eleven conclusions from other manuscripts whose Chaucerian authorship is doubtful.

The record models the edition as a composite so users can distinguish the securely attributed core through Part II.40 from conclusions II.41–51 supplied from three other manuscripts. This is an authorship and textual-boundary distinction, not merely a change of physical witness.

Skeat's edition appends eleven conclusions from other manuscripts whose Chaucerian authorship is doubtful.
